source.title: Replaying the game: hypnagogic images in normals and amnesics.

confidence_rationale: Provides human behavioral evidence that novel perceptual-motor experience is preferentially replayed at sleep onset (hypnagogic stage), even in amnesic patients without functional declarative memory -- suggesting the replay mechanism is automatic and driven by novelty/learning-load rather than by explicit memory formation. Directly relevant to MECH-180 as evidence that the sleep system selectively processes novel experience. Confidence is moderate because (1) the study measures hypnagogic imagery reports, a subjective proxy for replay rather than neural measurement, (2) SWA power, spindle density, and hippocampal replay rate are not measured, and (3) the amnesic finding is theoretically interesting but the n=3 amnesics is very small.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Participants who played Tetris for the first time reported stereotypical visual game images at sleep onset; amnesic patients who could not recall playing the game still reported such images, indicating replay at sleep onset is driven by experiential exposure rather than explicit memory.
mapping.ree_translation: Novel perceptual-motor experience preferentially targets the hypnagogic replay process, consistent with MECH-180's claim that novel environments and high-MEL episodes upregulate the learning drive component of sleep. The amnesic finding suggests the upregulation is automatic -- driven by the MEL-encoding process rather than by conscious recognition of novelty.
mapping.mapping_caveat: This study measures hypnagogic imagery (sleep onset, stage 1/2 transition), not slow-wave sleep replay, REM replay, SWA power, or spindle density -- the primary endpoints of MECH-180. The mapping to MECH-180 is conceptually valid (novel experience preferentially engages sleep-phase processing) but the specific neural endpoints in the claim are not measured. The amnesic patients had bilateral medial temporal lobe damage, so their Tetris imagery likely reflects non-hippocampal sensory/motor reactivation rather than hippocampal replay per se.
mapping.source_context: Human participants played Tetris for the first time (experiment group), previously (experienced group), or were amnesic patients (n=3). At sleep onset, participants were prompted to report any visual images. Novel players reported Tetris imagery at high rates; amnesics reported imagery at similar rates despite no recall of playing.

confidence_components.notes: Source quality is good: Science, controlled design with amnesic comparison group, behaviorally rigorous. Mapping fidelity is moderate -- the study establishes the novel-experience-to-sleep-replay link in humans but at the hypnagogic stage, not SWS/SWA/spindle endpoints that MECH-180 primarily targets. Transfer risk is moderate because hypnagogic imagery is a different phenomenon from SWS slow-wave or spindle upregulation, and the amnesic finding specifically argues against hippocampal dependence.
